item.title: concordant Crop Sequence Boundaries
item.description:
The concordant Crop Sequence Boundaries is a field boundary dataset which is an improved version of the Crop Sequence boundaries which relies on the similarity of the crop sequences to merge field fragments together, producing more stable field boundaries through time.

If you want to try using the dataset, you can download it here. I would not suggest its use for areas which have poor crop detection in the Cropland Data Layer, everywhere else should be fairly ok. I would suggest to use the field boundaries from Yan and Roy 2016 to confirm your results.

item.title: Cyclic Crop Rotations
item.description: One of the difficulties of doing crop rotation analysis is that you’re given a crop sequence that you need to interpret as a crop rotation. Perhaps the crop rotation changes through time and perhaps even changes the length of the crop rotation. People use a plethora of different methods to analyze crop sequences which I add my tool to.

item.title: About Me
item.description:
Goodday.

You have stumbled upon the website of Herbert.

I do things.

Research Realm:

I do some different pieces of research. Two questions I think about regularly are:

  • How do we improve data quality and data aggregation in Agricultural settings?
  • How do we model discrete actions and behaviors discretely?

Mostly, I think about this in terms of agricultural data, but will venture to other places if inspired.

Teaching Realm:

I’ve taught various courses in the past including:

item.title: Kokun: The Girl From the West by Nahoko Uehashi
item.description: Uehashi provides an analysis of empire and agriculture in this fantasy land, asking questions which are similar to those posed by James Scott in Against the Grain. The questions of the state and their role in agrarian systems and enforcing specific agrarian systems above others.

item.title: Against the Grain by James Scott
item.description: James Scott gives a foray into a somewhat speculative agriculture of pre-history of Europe and West Asia. The driving premise is that the ‘pre-historical’ agriculture was fairly ecologically stable and widely practiced at the same time as historical agricultural systems. Because the institutions of cities and early states allowed for maintance of records, our knowledge of historical agriculural systems is biased to preserving agricultural methods which supported cities and states.
